preamble: The shift from the voiced alveolar lateral fricative [ɮ] to the voiced alveolar lateral approximant [l] is a process of de-fricativization. This weakening occurs when the narrow constriction required for lateral friction is widened into a laminar approximant, often driven by contact with languages lacking the fricative lateral.

examples.note: Traditional [ɮ] is shifting toward a plain [l] due to intensive contact with Mandarin.
Early Arabic voiced lateral fricatives were adapted as lateral approximants when borrowed into Spanish.

sources: Ladefoged, P., & Maddieson, I. (1996). The Sounds of the World's Languages.
Hock, H. H. (1991). Principles of Historical Linguistics.
